senses.definition: A person who often dreams or spends time in imagination, especially in a romantic or idealistic way.
examples: He has always been a reveur, lost in his own thoughts.
The novelist describes herself as a quiet reveur who watches the world carefully.

senses.definition: Describing someone or something that looks dreamy, thoughtful, or lost in imagination.
examples: She gave him a reveur smile and looked away.
There is a reveur quality to his music that many fans love.

senses.definition: Plural of reveur: people who often dream or live in their imagination, especially in a romantic or idealistic way.
examples: The club is a place for reveurs who love poetry and art.
In his stories, the city belongs to the night-time reveurs who wander its streets.

senses.definition: A woman who often dreams or spends time in imagination, especially in a romantic or idealistic way.
examples: The film follows a lonely reveuse searching for meaning in the city.
As a child she was a shy reveuse, always reading or drawing.

senses.definition: Describing a woman or a feminine style that seems dreamy, thoughtful, or lost in imagination.
examples: She wore a reveuse expression as she watched the rain.
The painting has a soft, reveuse quality.

senses.definition: Plural of reveuse: women who often dream or live in their imagination, especially in a romantic or idealistic way.
examples: The novel tells the stories of several young reveuses in Paris.
The cafe was full of artists and reveuses sketching in their notebooks.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the nature of repeated echoes or vibrations, especially of sound.
examples: The poem used reveverberatory images of bells and drums.
Critics noted the reveverberatory power of the final chorus.

senses.definition: Vibrating again or sending out repeated vibrations or echoes.
examples: The revibrant strings filled the hall with sound.
He described the revibrant echoes of the distant thunder.

senses.definition: To vibrate again or repeatedly, especially by sending back sound or movement.
examples: The notes seemed to revibrate through the empty church.
The impact made the metal revibrate for several seconds.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of revibrate: vibrated again or repeatedly, especially by sending back sound or movement.
examples: The hall revibrated with applause after the concert.
Her words revibrated in his mind long after she had left.

senses.definition: Third person singular of revibrate: vibrates again or repeatedly, especially by sending back sound or movement.
examples: The sound revibrates through the canyon walls.
His name revibrates in local legends.

senses.definition: Present participle of revibrate: vibrating again or repeatedly, especially by sending back sound or movement.
examples: Revibrating strings filled the dark theater.
The revibrating metal made a low humming sound.

senses.definition: The action of vibrating again, or the repeated echo of a sound or movement.
examples: The revibration of the drumbeats could be felt in the floor.
They measured the revibration of the structure after the shock.

senses.definition: Relating to or caused by revibration or repeated vibration.
examples: The revibrational effects of the machinery were carefully studied.
Revibrational forces can damage tall buildings over time.

senses.definition: To make someone a victim again, especially after they have already suffered harm or abuse.
examples: Insensitive questions can revictimize people who have survived trauma.
The scam revictimized families who had already lost their homes.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of revictimize: was made a victim again or was harmed again after earlier harm.
examples: She felt revictimized by the way the media told her story.
Many people are revictimized when they report the crime.

senses.definition: Describing someone who has been made a victim again, especially by unfair treatment or procedures.
examples: Revictimized witnesses often lose trust in the justice system.
The report focused on the experiences of revictimized children.

senses.definition: Third person singular of revictimize: makes someone a victim again, especially after they have already suffered harm.
examples: This policy revictimizes people who report harassment.
The insensitive coverage revictimizes the families involved.

senses.definition: Present participle of revictimize: making someone a victim again, especially through unfair treatment or questioning.
examples: They changed the procedures to avoid revictimizing witnesses.
Revictimizing people with aggressive interviews can stop them from seeking help.

senses.definition: Victorious again, especially after having already won before.
examples: The team returned as revictorious champions.
Her revictorious campaign surprised many critics.

senses.definition: A second or later victory, especially after an earlier success.
examples: The party’s revictory confirmed its popularity.
Fans celebrated the club’s revictory in the final.

senses.definition: To supply a person, ship, or place with food and other necessary supplies again.
examples: They stopped at the port to revictual the fleet before the long voyage.
The explorers returned to revictual and rest.

senses.definition: To take on new supplies of food and necessities again, especially for a journey.
examples: The ship revictualed at Cape Town before sailing east.
They revictualed in the last town before crossing the desert.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of revictual: was supplied again with food and other necessary supplies.
examples: The ships revictualed in the bay and then continued north.
After they had revictualed, the troops marched on.

senses.definition: The act of supplying ships, soldiers, or travellers with fresh food and necessary supplies again.
examples: The revictualing of the fleet took several days.
Revictualing was difficult during the long winter campaign.

senses.definition: Present participle of revictual: supplying someone or something again with food and other necessary supplies.
examples: They are revictualing the ships before the storm arrives.
Crews spent the day revictualing and repairing equipment.

senses.definition: British spelling of revictualed: was supplied again with food and other necessary supplies.
examples: The vessel revictualled in the nearest safe harbor.
Once revictualled, the regiment advanced into the hills.

senses.definition: British spelling of revictualing: the act of supplying ships, soldiers, or travellers with fresh food and necessary supplies again.
examples: The revictualling of the naval base was carefully planned.
Severe weather delayed revictualling for several weeks.

senses.definition: British spelling of the present participle revictualing: supplying someone or something again with food and other necessary supplies.
examples: They were revictualling the ships throughout the night.
Crews spent days revictualling before the fleet left port.

senses.definition: The act or process of supplying food and other necessary supplies again, especially to soldiers or ships.
examples: The revictualment of the fortress was essential for its survival.
Delayed revictualment left the troops short of food.

senses.definition: Food and other necessary supplies taken on again, especially for a journey or for troops.
examples: They loaded fresh revictuals before leaving the port.
The revictuals included dried meat, grain, and water.

senses.definition: In some card games, to increase a bet in response to an opponent’s bet.
examples: He decided to revie, pushing more chips into the pot.
You may revie if you think your hand is strong enough.

senses.definition: To compete with or answer a challenge in return.
examples: The smaller company tried to revie with a bold offer.
His rivals soon revied with their own proposals.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of revie: increased a bet or answered a challenge in return.
examples: He revied and forced the other players to decide quickly.
Several bidders revied as the auction continued.

senses.definition: Third person singular of revie: increases a bet or answers a challenge in return.
examples: If he revies, the stakes will double.
The company revies whenever a rival lowers its prices.

senses.definition: An opinion about a book, film, product, or event, written or spoken for other people.
examples: I read a review of the movie before watching it.
The restaurant got very good reviews online.

senses.definition: The process of carefully checking or thinking about something again to improve it or to make a decision.
examples: The budget is under review at the moment.
We will do a review of our safety procedures.

senses.definition: To carefully look at or think about something again to improve it or to make a decision.
examples: The teacher asked us to review our notes before the test.
The committee will review the proposal next week.

senses.definition: To write or give your opinion about a book, film, product, or event.
examples: She reviews films for a popular website.
I was asked to review a new language course.

senses.definition: The quality of something that can be examined or judged again, especially by a court or authority.
examples: The judge questioned the reviewability of that administrative decision.
Some actions of the agency fall outside judicial reviewability.

senses.definition: Able to be examined, judged, or changed, especially by a court or authority.
examples: The contract is not reviewable after it is signed.
Only certain decisions are reviewable by the higher court.

senses.definition: The activity, amount, or body of reviews, especially written comments about books, films, or products.
examples: There has been a lot of reviewage about the new series.
Online reviewage can strongly influence sales.

senses.definition: The act of looking at or considering something again.
examples: The policy is subject to reviewal every five years.
Any reviewal of the contract must be agreed by both parties.

senses.definition: Plural of reviewal: several acts of looking at or considering something again.
examples: Regular reviewals of the plan kept it up to date.
There have been multiple reviewals of the safety rules.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of review: looked at or examined something again to improve it or make a decision.
examples: We reviewed the report before sending it.
The manager reviewed all the applications carefully.

senses.definition: Looked at or judged a book, film, product, or event and gave an opinion about it.
examples: She reviewed the concert for the local newspaper.
The new smartphone has been widely reviewed online.

senses.definition: A person who writes or gives opinions about books, films, products, or events.
examples: The reviewer praised the actors but criticized the script.
I work as a book reviewer for an online magazine.

senses.definition: A person who checks work, decisions, or documents, especially in a formal process.
examples: Each article is read by at least one independent reviewer.
The reviewer suggested several changes to the grant application.

senses.definition: An old or humorous word for a female reviewer.
examples: The magazine’s famous revieweress became a cultural icon.
He jokingly called himself a revieweress of cafes and bars.

senses.definition: Plural of reviewer: people who write or give opinions about books, films, products, events, or formal work.
examples: Many reviewers called the novel a modern classic.
The journal relies on expert reviewers to judge new research.

senses.definition: Present participle of review: looking at or examining something again to improve it or make a decision.
examples: We are currently reviewing our hiring policies.
She spent the afternoon reviewing for the exam.

senses.definition: Writing or giving opinions about books, films, products, or events.
examples: He has been reviewing games for over ten years.
She enjoys reviewing new restaurants on her blog.

senses.definition: Having the style or quality of a review, especially in giving opinions or judgments.
examples: The article has a light, reviewish tone.
Her comments were brief and rather reviewish.

senses.definition: Without reviews or written opinions, especially about a book, film, or product.
examples: Many reviewless books get lost on the website.
He was nervous about buying a reviewless product.

senses.definition: Plural of review: written or spoken opinions about books, films, products, or events.
examples: The film received excellent reviews.
I always read customer reviews before I buy something online.

senses.definition: Third person singular of review: looks at or examines something again, or gives an opinion about it.
examples: The manager reviews each report on Friday.
She reviews new plays for a national newspaper.

senses.definition: The act of making something alive again or giving it new life, often used figuratively.
examples: The festival led to a cultural revification of the old town.
Some critics saw the project as a revification of traditional crafts.

senses.definition: To give new strength or energy to something or someone.
examples: A short holiday would revigor the tired team.
The reforms helped to revigor the local economy.

senses.definition: To give new energy, strength, or enthusiasm to something or someone.
examples: The new coach managed to revigorate the team.
They introduced several changes to revigorate the company’s image.

senses.definition: The act of giving new energy, strength, or enthusiasm to something or someone.
examples: The project led to a revigoration of the city center.
Exercise can bring a sense of revigoration after a long day.

senses.definition: The state of having new strength, energy, or health.
examples: After a week of rest he returned to work with revigour.
The revigour of the housing market surprised analysts.

senses.definition: To speak about someone or something in a very angry and insulting way.
examples: The politician was widely reviled for his racist comments.
They reviled him as a traitor to the cause.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of revile: was spoken about in a very angry and insulting way.
examples: He was reviled by the crowd for his decision.
Once admired, she is now reviled in her home country.

senses.definition: Describing someone or something that many people hate and speak about in a very insulting way.
examples: He became one of the most reviled figures in modern history.
The law is deeply reviled by civil rights groups.

senses.definition: The act of speaking about someone or something in a very angry and insulting way, or the insulting words themselves.
examples: She had suffered years of public revilement.
Revilement from his former fans hurt him deeply.
